text: may wish to do so) now and again does not necessarily serve as a question mark to normality; often quite the contrary. Fifth, by thus delimiting liminality, Turner underplayed the extent to which liminal moments or liminal experiences might be equally present in political or social transformations, i.e. outside ‘culture’, in the narrower understanding of that term. Here Turner was certainly influenced by the Parsonian version of anthropology as dealing with ‘symbol systems’ or ‘comparative symbology’. In a general sense, Turner was drawn towards performance genres and literature, and had relatively little interaction with political scientists. Despite Turner’s frequent references to ‘complex society’, and his many allusions to the relevance of liminality for ‘macropolitics’ (1988: 91), and despite his earlier engagement with political anthropology (see Swartz, Turner and Tuden 1966) his work remained largely a-political in character. The consequence is that we today know of Victor Turner if we study theatre and art; we know practically nothing of Turner if we study politics or sociology. 7 And that is not such an entirely obvious division of labour, given the potential of the liminality concept and its centrality for understanding transition and change. Given the very complex history of the reception of both Arnold van Gennep’s and Victor Turner’s works, let us be quite explicit about how, from our point of view, we ought not engage the liminality concept: namely as a celebration of human creativity and freedom, connected to normlessness and going-beyond existing traditions that bound and limit human beings. In short, Turner’s own identification of his project as a postmodern liberation from modern conditions is not a position that should be endorsed. Turner’s notion of the liminoid is not an analytic step forward; quite the contrary. The notion of the liminoid has allowed for an indiscriminate application of liminality. On that account we ought to be slightly conservative and return to the starting point of van Gennep’s analysis: liminality has to do with transition, the ritual forms such transitions take, and the way in which transitions shape both persons and communities. If it is not about transition, it simply is not about liminality. This, however, does not mean that all transitions go smoothly. They in fact do not. Put differently, Turner’s work is crucial, but it needs to be differently grounded in order to sustain its theoretical potential. This is not done by pulling Turner’s 6 And it is not difficult to say when: such single moments can be explosive and life- altering when we already find ourselves in a liminal moment, open (fragile, receptive, vulnerable) to external input. 7 The book entitled Political Anthropology, having Turner as one of the editors, was clearly an embryonic attempt to inject a processual approach into the study of politics, but the liminal nature of modern politics was not even hinted at here (see Swartz, Turner and Tuden 1966). Liminality and the Modern 86 terminology back into a Durkheimian framework – as infelicitously suggested by Rothenbühler (1988). Turner himself (not to mention van Gennep!) would have revolted at seeing his work caged by the -isms he had so carefully and explicitly liberated himself from. Rather, in order to open up Turner’s work we must think it through from within, and start by singling out the somewhat overlooked and not always clearly expressed political aspects of his work; and then integrate those dimensions with parallel or complementary efforts to capture social and political processes of change and transition. This will be the focus of the chapter that follows. But this effort must take into account Turner’s late encounter with philosophy, and especially, with the work of Wilhelm Dilthey. Let us briefly consider this encounter before we move on. Turner Encounters Dilthey: The Tying Together of Thought and Experience in Liminality For Turner, the study of liminality was a study of human experience. In his late career, this brought him into contact not only with novel empirical phenomena, but also with a philosophical dimension. He was no expert, but he jumped into it, trying to make his own contribution. Like almost any other brilliant thinker, Turner came to take a strong interest in etymology. In a posthumously published 1980 conference presentation, Turner ventures into the etymology of experience through the PIE (proto-indo-European) root *per (Turner 1985: 226). Etymology is not a study of dead letters, standing in contrast to what van Gennep called ‘living facts’. Etymology actually assumes its full importance exactly within a processual approach. This is because words are not simply ‘derived’ from earlier usages that have been handed over and/or changed over time. Words are themselves condensed symbolic forms, derived from real human experiences; in fact, most of our crucial terms and words developed in what Koselleck has called sattelzeit periods, in liminal periods. This was what van Gennep had intuited from a very early age, and that is why he linked the notion of ‘marks’ with an attempt to revise existing theories of language. Sometimes things happen for which we have no proper words: and that is when we start looking for such words. Words are, in a certain sense, distilled symbolic forms that take shape during a period of transition, in which meanings become ‘fluid’ and therefore open to elaboration. A detailed analysis of the meanings derived from the PIE root *per confirms Turner’s view that experience is intimately tied to liminality and the passing of a threshold. We can here again rely on the summary provided by Szakolczai (2009: 149–50), building on Turner’s own reflection but adding further detail. Turner stresses that the primary meaning of *per is not simply passage, but successful completion of a passage. *Per derivatives also capture the intense emotion that accompanies such attempts, as indicated by terms like ‘fear’ or ‘peril’. A successful passage also assumes a particular ordering in which somebody goes ahead, showing the way or blazing the trail so that others can follow, ‘imitating’ Liminality Rediscovered: With Victor Turner and Beyond 87 him, which corresponds to the need for masters
